coredata.dc:title: A novel and selective multi-emission chemiluminescence system for the quantification of deltamethrin in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Yahyai, Iman Al
Hassanzadeh, Javad
Al-Lawati, Haider A.J.
coredata.dc:description: A paper-based analytical device (PADs) combined with a powerful chemiluminescence (CL) system was established for the determination of deltamethrin (DM), based on the enhancing effect of polyphosphate (PP) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-KMnO4 CL reaction. A possible mechanism for the obtained emission was proposed using the CL spectra, fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible patterns. The reaction of KMnO4 and GQDs can lead to generation of GQDs in excited state (GQDs*), which can emit at 490 nm. Interestingly, PP changes the CL mechanism and the main emitter becomes Mn2+ instead of GQDs leading to a strong emission at 695 nm. Furthermore, the obtained multi-emission CL system was examined for analytical applications. The initial experiments showed that the amplified CL emission of the GQDs-KMnO4 system was selectively quenched in the presence of trace levels of DM, probably due to its effective interaction with GQDs or reaction with KMnO4. This observation led to a facile, reliable and sensitive PADs-CL probe, developed for the determination of DM residue in food samples. Using this CL system and under the optimized experimental conditions, the generated signal is decreased by increasing DM concentration in the range of 0.3−10 μg mL-1 with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μg mL-1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Paper-based analytical device
Nanomaterial
Deltamethrin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref vitae

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank Sultan Qaboos University for the grant and financial support of this work. The authors would also like to thank Mr. Abdul Rahman Al Nabhani for helping with the TEM experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic image of the detection of DM by PP-GQDs-KMnO4 CL based system.
(a) Schematic illustration of PAD-CL probe used for DM detection. (b) Photographs of (I)3D-printed chip used as paper holder (II) paper-based device.

TEM (A) image for synthesized GQDs; X-ray diffraction (XRD) (B) pattern and FT-IR (C) spectrum of GQDs; ﬂuorescence emission with Different excitation wavelength (a-j: 320,340,360,380,400,420,440,460,480 nm) for the synthesized GQDs (10 times diluted).

A) Kinetic profiles for proposed CL systems after injection of 1 mM KMnO4 [HCl = 1 M, PP = 8%, m/v, GQDs = 10 times diluted, DM = 5 μg mL−1]; B) CL spectra for PP-GQDs-KMnO4 reaction at different wavelengths (350-850 nm) [HCl = 0.5 M, PP = 8%, m/v, GQDs = 5 times diluted, DM = 10 μg mL−1] ; C) CL spectra for different mixed solutions [HCl = 1 M, PP = 8%, m/v, GQDs = 10 times diluted, DM = 5 μg mL−1].

Kinetic profiles for PP-GQDs-KMnO4 CL system in the presence of different concentrations of DM, the inset shows the linear calibration graph for the determination of DM under optimum condition; a-f: 0, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 5, 7 and 10 μg mL−1 DM.

Change in the CL intensity ( Δ I ) of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system in the presence of some compounds; the inset shows the CL intensity of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system in the presence of some compounds (with or without 5 μg mL−1 DM) using the optimum condition, the concentration of each examined compound is 5 μg mL−1.
Obtained results for the determination of DM in spiked apple juice samples by developed CL method.

head.ce:title: A novel and selective multi-emission chemiluminescence system for the quantification of deltamethrin in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Sultan Qaboos University, Box 36, Al-Khod 123, Oman
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Sultan Qaboos University, Box 36, Al-Khod 123, Oman

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A paper-based analytical device (PADs) combined with a powerful chemiluminescence (CL) system was established for the determination of deltamethrin (DM), based on the enhancing effect of polyphosphate (PP) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-KMnO4 CL reaction. A possible mechanism for the obtained emission was proposed using the CL spectra, fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible patterns. The reaction of KMnO4 and GQDs can lead to generation of GQDs in excited state (GQDs*), which can emit at 490 nm. Interestingly, PP changes the CL mechanism and the main emitter becomes Mn2+ instead of GQDs leading to a strong emission at 695 nm. Furthermore, the obtained multi-emission CL system was examined for analytical applications. The initial experiments showed that the amplified CL emission of the GQDs-KMnO4 system was selectively quenched in the presence of trace levels of DM, probably due to its effective interaction with GQDs or reaction with KMnO4. This observation led to a facile, reliable and sensitive PADs-CL probe, developed for the determination of DM residue in food samples. Using this CL system and under the optimized experimental conditions, the generated signal is decreased by increasing DM concentration in the range of 0.3−10 μg mL-1 with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μg mL-1.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, pesticides play a crucial role throughout the world. Their contributions to the economy (via increasing in both agricultural yield and food production) and to upgrading health level has led to unreasonable use of pesticides [1]. Pyrethroids is a type of synthetic insecticides that has been applied in many fields to control insect pests [2]. It is characterized by its high selectivity toward the target species compared to the other types of insecticides [3]. Since the application of restrictions to pesticide sales of organophosphorus (OP) insecticides, pyrethroids came as an alternative option for many consumers where the rate of pyrethroid use increased significantly especially in agricultural-based countries [4,5]. Deltamethrin (DM), (S)-alpha-cyano-3-phenoxybenzyl-(1R)-cis-3-(2,2-di-bromovinyl)-2,2-dimethylcyclopropane carboxylate, is an effective chemical that belongs to the Type II synthetic pyrethroids [6,7]. It is widely used to safeguard agricultural crops and fish against wide range of parasites (i.e. lice, flies and ticks) in addition to controlling human disease vectors [8,9]. Despite its advantages, prolonged exposure to even trace levels of this pollutant causes several environmental and health risks. It harms the society through oral ingestion of contaminated water and food stuff [5,10]. Exposure to DM leads to generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that causes liver dysfunction, protein damage and genotoxicity [11,12].

A bountiful number of analytical techniques have been developed and used for monitoring DM in food and environmental samples, mainly encompassing chromatographic techniques coupled with diverse types of detection methods [13–15]. Other methodologies including electrochemical [16], spectroscopic [17] and immunoassay-based methods [18] have been also reported for the quantification of deltamethrin. In spite of the high sensitivity of the methods mentioned above, it cannot be denied the truth that most of them are time-consuming, expensive, labor intensive, relatively high solvent consumption and require skilled technicians, which prompted researchers to replace them with miniaturized systems [19].

Recently introduced miniaturized systems are attractive alternatives to replace the traditional bench-top analytical systems. Miniaturization may bring many benefits such as high surface area-to-volume ratio, reduction of dead volume, lower solvent consumption, faster and higher analysis throughput, as well as portability of the analytical system that overcome the requirement of laboratory bench-scale equipment for sample preparation and injection, reaction and detection in conventional methods. Portability is desirable for applications that should be performed in situ like on-site testing of pollutants in environmental and food samples. Microfluidic platforms including papers and cloths and lab-on-chip are examples for miniaturized analytical devices [20]. Nowadays, PADs have attracted increased attention compared to other types of microfluidic platforms. Due to their unique features such as ease and low-cost of fabrication, strong capillary action and environmentally friendly, PADs have been applied successfully in many applications including clinical diagnosis, food quality control and environmental monitoring [21]. Different detection techniques used in PADs, namely: colorimetric [22], fluorometric [23], electrochemical [24], spectrometry [25] and chemiluminescence (CL) [26] detections.

Among the other methods, CL detection has gained much interest as a detection technique for PADs resulting from its ease of coupling [26]. CL can be defined as the emission of light resulting from a chemical reaction [19]. Because of its simplicity, low cost and high sensitivity and selectivity, CL-based methods have been recently well developed for monitoring pesticides [27]. The limit of detection (LOD) can be improved by this way since no external source of light is needed and thus minimizing the background signal. Also, the volumes of waste organic solvents in separation setups can be reduced [19].

The sensitivity and stability of traditional CL systems still need to be improved. Incorporation of nanomaterials can intensify the resulting CL signals [28]. Among the nanomaterials, quantum dots (QDs), with sizes in the range 1−10 nm, play an efficient role in CL systems for determination of organic and inorganic materials because of their unique merits [29]. QDs possess unique optical and electronic properties including broad absorption band, narrow and symmetric emission peaks, high-emission quantum yields, and good chemical and optical stability [30]. GQDs is an example of QDs, consisting of graphene sheets with sizes smaller than 100 nm [31]. Moreover, GQDs possess high photo-stability against photo-bleaching and blinking. In addition, quantum confinement, tunable band gap and high surface area are resulting from their small particle size [32]. Due to their low toxicity and biocompatibility, the researchers paid special attention to GQDs.

Herein, PADs combined with a new and powerful CL system was developed for facile, reliable and sensitive detection of DM pesticide. In this approach, GQDs has been utilized in CL analysis of pesticides. It was synthesized by a simple and green rout using safe precursors. A paper cutting fabrication technique was used to make the simple square shaped paper device. The overlying purpose of paper in this work is to develop inexpensive, eco-friendly analytical tool suitable for pesticides analysis. The applied CL system is based on the increasing effect of polyphosphate (PP) on the CL signal of GQDs-KMnO4, which can generate emission at two different wavelengths. In an acidic environment, KMnO4 can oxidize GQDs producing GQDs* in its excited state, which leads to light emission after its relaxation (490 nm). In addition, by adding PP, it can improve the generation of Mn(II)* emitter species by stabilizing the related intermediates, leading to a strong emission at 695 nm. This effect remarkably improved the CL emission intensity by GQDs-KMnO4 reaction (Fig. 1 ). GQDs with different sources were examined in this system and the best one was used for the experiments. Furthermore, as a useful analytical application of the developed paper-based CL system, it was used for the detection of DM, as a perilous compound. In the presence of DM, the CL intensity of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 was diminished because of the consumption of oxidant by the analyte (DM) and/or the interaction of analyte with GQDs. Various experimental conditions were optimized like the concentrations and volumes of various reagents and GQDs and the size of detection paper. Finally, the method showed great reproducibility and accuracy for the determination of DM in apple juice.

All reagents were of analytical grade, and dilutions were performed using deionized water (Millipore, MilliQ water system). Ascorbic acid, uric acid, sucrose, l-leucine, l-cysteine, histidine, urea, and sulfuric acid were purchased from BDH (Poole, England). β-d-(+)-glucose, thiamine (vitamin B1), hydrochloric acid, acetonitrile, methanol, deltamethrin and other pesticides used in this work were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Sodium polyphosphate was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Potassium permanganate was purchased from Qualigens (Mumbai, India). Lactose was purchased from Grainland International Ltd (United Kingdom). Ortho-phosphoric acid was purchased from Surechem products Ltd (England).

KMnO4 stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared by dissolving 7.9 mg of potassium permanganate in 5.0 mL of deionized water. Sodium polyphosphate (8%, m/v) was prepared by dissolving 2.0 g of sodium polyphosphate in 25 mL deionized water. Stock solution (100 μg mL−1) of DM was prepared using 100 % acetonitrile and further dilutions were performed using DI water.

For the characterization of the prepared GQDs, the chemical structure was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Agilent Technologies, Cary 630 FTIR, USA). The GQDs surface morphology and size were investigated via a field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM) (JEM 2100 JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. XRD pattern of GQDs was obtained using D5000 X-ray diffractometer (Siemens, Germany), with an exciting source of Cu Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å). For the CL studies, photomultiplier tube (PMT, Hamamatsu H7155-2, Japan) with 8 mm detection area was used. Fluorescence spectra was recorded by Spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, RF 5301 PC, Japan). Whereas, UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1650 PC, Japan) was used to investigate the absorption spectra. The syringe and pump were obtained from Harvard (USA) and KSD scientific (USA), respectively.

GQDs has been utilized in CL analysis of DM. It was synthesized by a simple and green route using safe precursors. In short, 2.0 g of urea with 4.0 g of lactose powder were added into a small beaker. The mixture was heated while stirring till melting. The heating process continued until the appearance of brownish color solution which indicated the formation of GQDs. After that, 25 mL of DI water was added to the mixture with continues stirring for ∼ 4 min. GQDs was kept for one day prior to use and diluted by 10 times. It is better to mention that the synthesis conditions for GQDs have been optimized to obtain maximum emission intensity.

A paper holder was designed using AutoCAD 2018 software and printed via a MiiCraft 125 3D printer to fit with the size of paper and setup used in this work. The resin for 3D holder printing used in this study was BV-007 Acrylate polymers obtained from Hsinchu Industrial Park, Hsinchu Country, Taiwan. A paper cutter was used to cut a chromatographic paper (Whatman, England) into small square (1cm × 1cm) pieces. A piece of paper was set in the paper holder where the CL reagents were added one by one. With micropipette, an appropriate volume of each reagent was transferred on the paper in the following sequence: blank or analyte (2 μL, 5 μg mL−1), HCl (2 μL, 1 M), PP (2 μL, 8%, m/v) and GQD (2 μL, 10 times diluted). The paper holder was placed directly above the PMT in a dark box. KMnO4 (10 μL, 100 μL min−1, 1 mM) was dropped onto the paper device through the injection hole on top of the setup by a pump (Fig. 2 ). For quantitative measurements, the total CL signal intensity at both wavelengths (490 and 695 nm) was obtained using photomultiplier tube (PMT). Data was obtained and processed using C8855-01 software. The determination of DM was carried out based on its quenching effect on the CL intensity using a calibration curve.

Commercial apple juices from two different companies (Al Marai and A’Safwaa) were bought from local supermarket. Each juice was filtered using KX Syring Filter, PTEF 25-mm, 0.45-μm, and diluted ten times with Milli-Q water. SPE procedure was performed using a Visiprep™-DL SPE vacuum manifold from Supelco (Bellefonte, PA, USA) with supelclean envi-18 SPE 500 mg (Supelco, USA) cartridges. SPE cartridges were preconditioned with 5 mL MeOH and 5 mL Milli-Q water before the pretreatment procedure. Then, 3 mL of apple juices spiked with (0.0, 0.5 and 3.0) μg mL−1 of DM were passed through the preconditioned cartridge. After loading the sample, 6 mL of Milli-Q water was passed to wash the cartridges. The retained analyte was eluted with 6 mL MeOH. The elution solvent was then evaporated to dryness under purging of N2 gas and the residues were redissolved in 1 mL of 10 % ACN in water and analyzed with developed method.

CL spectra were plotted for the solution-based CL system using a spectrofluorometer and a two-way flow-based system. First channel contained acidic KMnO4 (1 mM in 0.5 M HCl), and the second one delivered PP (8%, m/v), GQD (5 times diluted) and deltamethrin (10 μg mL−1). The solutions were mixed together and directed into the flow-cell, which placed in front of the detector. The radiation source was turned off and the emission monochromator (slit: 25 nm) was scanned in the range of 350−850 nm. The obtained emission intensity was recorded versus the wavelength.

The CL spectra were also recorded for the developed paper-based CL system, using the spectrofluorometer. The paper system was prepared by adding the appropriate volumes of analyte, HCl, PP and GQD, respectively, as mentioned in section 2.3. The paper holder was placed in the sample area of spectrofluorometer, in front of the slit of the emission monochromator. KMnO4 was injected onto the paper using a syringe pump, and the emitted CL intensity was recorded in different wavelengths, adjusted by emission monochromator. The obtained intensities were plotted against the wavelengths.

The morphology and size of the prepared GQDs were assessed by TEM method. The TEM image (Fig. 3 A) revealed the spherical shape and nearly monodisperse nanoparticles with size distribution lower than 10 nm. XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 3B) under investigation confirmed the amorphous nature of synthesized GQDs. A broad diffraction peak at 2Ɵ of 18° was observed, which correspond to the (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic structure [33].

The presence of different functional groups in GQDs was proved using FT-IR method as shown in (Fig. 3C). As presented in FT-IR spectrum, the peaks located near 1632 and 1461 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and CC, respectively. In addition, an intense and broad band was formed at 3323 cm -1 corresponding to OH stretching vibration. Other bands in the range of (1150−1050 cm−1) are assigned to the OH bending, CN and CO stretching vibrations resulting from ether, ester and carboxylic acid.

In addition, the prepared GQDs represents fluorescence emission peaks using different excitation wavelengths. As seen in Fig. 3D, the emission peaks of the GQDs shift with varying excitation, exhibiting excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. The strongest emission peak is located at 455 nm upon excitation with wavelength of 390 nm. Therefore, 390 nm was chosen as the best excitation wavelength for fluorescence experimentations.

The kinetic profiles of the developed CL system of potassium permanganate in the presence of GQDs and PP are presented in Fig. 4 A. The curve a describes a weak emission generated by PP alone, due to the loss of any suitable reductant to efficiently create CL emitters. The observed emission is probably due to the reaction of permanganate with HCl, producing some excited Mn(II) (Mn(II)*) species. However, GQDs acts as a potent reductant for KMnO4 and causes a relatively strong CL emission (curve b), which can be related to the effective creation of Mn(II)* and/or the generation of excited GQDs (GQDs*) [34]. More surprisingly, polyphosphate improved the CL intensity of GQDs-KMnO4 and made a more intensifying CL emission, as shown in Fig. 4A (curve c). Based on this observation, a more intensive CL emission was produced using PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system in acidic condition. The developed CL system was tested for the analysis of DM, which quenched the induced CL emission (Fig. 4A, curve d).

An appropriate description for the possible CL reaction pathway can be performed by identifying the emitting species in the reaction. Fig. 4B and C illustrate the CL spectra of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system in the solution-based and paper-based detections, respectively. It is clear that the main emission of GQDs-KMnO4 reaction is located at 490 nm, which is very close to their fluorescence emission wavelength. KMnO4 acts as an oxidant and creates some holes in the GQDs. The change in the absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the presence of KMnO4 confirmed their efficient reaction (Fig. S1A, curve e and g) and (Fig. S1B), respectively. The radiative recombination of these holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs can result in energy release as CL emission. So, GQDs* produced by energy-transfer annihilation, probably act as the main emitters. Also, a weak emission was observed for this system around 695 nm, may related to the Mn2+* species produced by reducing of MnO4 − in the presence of GQDs. By adding PP, the situation changed; the CL spectrum showed two strong emission peaks around 490 nm and 695 nm. The first one, assigned to the GQDs*, had a same intensity as obtained in the absence of PP. However, the emission at 695 nm was remarkably intensified by adding PP. This observation can strongly demonstrate the great enhancing effect of PP on the Mn2+* emission. PP has been known as a common enhancer for the KMnO4 CL systems. It can improve the CL emission by stabilizing the reaction intermediates, which can produce Mn(II)*. The most well-known intermediate for the creation of this emitter is Mn(III) species. The more stable Mn(III) can cause the creation of a high amount of excited Mn(II), and so increase the CL emission at 695 nm [35]. Also, PP can form protective “cage-like’’ structures around the emitter which reduce loss of energy through non-radiative energy dissipation [35]. On the other hand, the emission at 490 nm is caused by excited GQDs, and intermediate Mn species have no notable effect on this emission. So, PP cannot improve the CL emission at 490 nm.

Superoxide anion radicals and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) have been also reported as the efficient electron injector to GQDs [36,37]. These radicals can act as reducing agents and react with GQDs, leading to the creation of GQDs
−. On the other hand, GQDs
+ can be also produced by MnO4 − in acidic condition. The combination of GQDs
− and GQDs
+ can form excited-state GQDs*, emitting at 490 nm. In order to investigate this mechanism, the CL emission was followed in the deoxygenized solutions. The CL emission showed a 22.6 % decrease after deoxygenation process by N2 bubbling for 10 min, demonstrating the role of dissolved oxygen on the CL emission of GQDs. Furthermore, effects of a common
OH scavenger (t-butyl alcohol) and O2 −
quencher (benzoquinone) were evaluated on the CL emission of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system. Adding t-butyl alcohol (0.2 mM, 2 μL) on the paper, loaded by CL reagents, decreased the CL intensity by 6.3 %, However, benzoquinone (0.3 mM, 2 μL) led to a remarkable diminishing effect and the CL intensity decreased by 28 %. The results confirmed the participation of ROS in the CL emission at 490 nm, and O2 −
radicals are the major species, which play an important role.

The overall CL reaction of the mentioned system can be described by the following equations:
(1) KMnO4 + H+ + GQDs + O2 → Mn2+* + GQDs*+ ROS
(2) ROS + GQDs → GQDs
−
(3) KMnO4 + H+ + GQDs → Mn2+* + GQDs
+
(4) GQDs
+ + GQDs
− → GQDs*
(5) GQDs* → GQDs + hυ (490 nm)
(6) Mn2+* → Mn2+ + hυ (695 nm)

The CL intensity of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 was effectively quenched in the presence of DM (Fig. 4). As it is clear in the CL spectra, DM decreases the CL intensity at 490 nm by 26 %, however, its quenching effect on the peak at 695 nm is notable and the CL intensity decreases by 64 % (Fig. 4C). On the other hand, the absorption intensity of KMnO4 was significantly reduced by the addition of DM with different concentrations as display in Fig. S1A (curve c and d), indicating the consumption of KMnO4 by the analyte. Also, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs were decreased by both DM and KMnO4 as appear in Fig. S1, respectively, and can be lowered further by the presence of both materials in the solution. Consequently, the main reasons of the diminishing effect of DM can be considered as follow: DM competes with GQDs to react with oxidant and so, the consumption of KMnO4 can result in the decrease of the CL intensity. On the other hand, the interaction of DM with GQDs can also occur and affect the CL intensity. The former effect is more important as observed in CL spectra (Fig. 4C).

A series of experiments was performed to select the optimum chemical and physical conditions for the CL reaction. Various experimental parameters were investigated including the volumes and mixing order of reagents, the size of paper, the type of the acid and its concentration and the concentrations of other reagents.

In this regard, various mixing order of reagents were investigated. The best CL response was obtained in the following order: Blank or analyte-HCl-PP-GQDs. Moreover, emissions of the proposed CL reactions involving different volumes of reagents in the range of 2−10 μL were monitored and 2 μL was recognized as the optimized volume of all reagents in paper. In addition, paper with sizes: 0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 1.7 cm were examined and gave the maximum intensity with paper size 1 cm. To select an acid for the proposed study, H2SO4, H3PO4 and HCl were tested with various concentrations in the range 0.5–2.5 M (see Fig. S2 A). Although the CL signal could be observed in all acidic mediums, 1 M of HCl, however, gave the strongest CL signal which was used for subsequent experiments.

Potassium permanganate was used as an oxidant in this work. The drop of KMnO4 was controlled and the optimum volume was found to be 10 μL at flow rate 100 μL min−1. The effect of its concentration was evaluated from 0.2 to1.5 mM and represented in Fig. S2B. According to this figure, gradually increasing in the CL signals was observed up to 1 mM and then decreased. Based on that, 1 mM of KMnO4 was used as optimum value during the experiments. Furthermore, sodium polyphosphate plays an important role in amplifying the CL emission from many reactions with permanganate [38]. Hence, the effect of the concentration of polyphosphate (1–8 %, m/v) was investigated and the appropriate CL response was achieved at (8%, m/v), see Fig. S2C. Finally, the dilution factor of GQDs solution on the CL emission was studied over the range of 5–20 times (Fig. S2D). High CL response was acquired when GQDs solution was diluted by 10 times.

Under the optimum conditions described above, the relationship between CL intensity of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 CL system and DM concentration was investigated. The corresponding calibration curve for the determination of DM in optimum condition was represented in Fig. 5 . The linear range of the proposed method was from 0.3 to 10.0 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9908) and regression equation as follows:
ΔI = -56953C + 1 × 106.
(Where ΔI = the difference between the CL emissions of proposed system in the absence and presence of DM; C = DM concentration in μg mL−1).
The LOD (3S) was found to be 0.15 μg mL−1. Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSD%) for three replicate determination of DM over the range studied were 0.49–1.4 %.

The above-mentioned results confirm the high sensitivity of the established method, great analytical range and the good repeatability. Table S1 represents a comparison between the analytical methods that were published for the determination of DM with this approach. It is clear that the developed system is characterized by its simplicity, rapidity, inexpensive instrumentation and low detection limit.

It is an important task to identify the selectivity of the method for DM in the presence of other foreign species in environmental and food samples. To assess the selectivity of the established procedure, the influence of different types of sugars, amino acids and vitamins were investigated. The responses of the CL reaction were monitored in the presence of 5.0 μg mL−1 DM and various quantities of the possible interfering compounds. Moreover, the effect of other types of pesticides on the developed system has been studied. The results in Fig. 6 shows the ignorable effects of these compounds and the acceptable eclectic for DM, which indicates the relatively great selectivity of the proposed method since the changes in CL responses caused by foreign species is less than 5 %. The high selectivity of developed CL system towards the DM can be probably related to the more efficient interaction of DM with GQDs and/or its rapid reaction with KMnO4. The slow reaction of other pesticides with KMnO4 cannot affect the CL emission.

The proposed method was applied for the determination of DM in commercial apple juices. Samples were prepared, as described in Section 2. Different concentrations of DM standard solution were added in the juice sample and then analyzed by developed method. The obtained results are displayed in Fig. 7 . It shows the high reliability of this detection system for the measurement of deltamethrin in real samples. As well, the high recoveries of validated method can be estimated by comparing the results of the standards with those of spiked samples.

In this study, a simple PADs-CL method was introduced for DM detection which supplies sufficient sensitivity without using an expensive CL reagent. GQDs was successfully synthesized by a simple and green route using safe and inexpensive precursors and characterized with different techniques. It showed high amplification for the CL emission of PP-GQDs-KMnO4 system. Therefore, an effective and reliable CL system was established based on PP-GQDs-KMnO4, which can emit in two different wavelength ranges. The mechanism of the reaction was also investigated by kinetic profiles and CL spectra. Furthermore, based on the quenching effect of DM on the emitted CL intensity, the proposed method was examined for the measurement of DM. Under the optimum conditions, DM could be determined with high sensitivity over a wide linear range and low detection limit down to 0.15 μg mL−1. Regarding to the obtained results, this method can be eligible for DM analysis at a minimal cost as a result of using small volumes of reagents. Ultimately, it was shown that the method was applicable for the determination of DM in food samples. The developed method can be considered as a new analytical detection system for on-site screening purposes and extended for other important analytes.

Iman Al Yahyai: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Javad Hassanzadeh: Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Haider A.J. Al-Lawati: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Sultan Qaboos University for the grant and financial support of this work. The authors would also like to thank Mr. Abdul Rahman Al Nabhani for helping with the TEM experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128927.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties and determination of pesticides in fruits and vegetables
TrAC Trends Anal. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Iman Al-Yahyai is a PhD student in the chemistry department- college of science at Sultan Qaboos university, under the supervision of Dr. Haider Al Lawatti. Her research interests are focused on microfluidic, AutoCAD chip designing and chemiluminescence detection of pesticides.

Javad Hassanzadeh received his PhD in Analytical Chemistry from University of Tabriz (Iran) in 2013. He is interested in chemiluminescence & fluorescence reactions and microfluidic analysis systems. He has published 48 papers in international ISI indexed Journals and 3 papers in ISC Journals. He currently works as a Post Doc researcher in the department of chemistry, college of science, SQU, under the supervision of Dr. Haider Al Lawati, focusing on the determination of antioxidants and sugars.

Haider A. J. Al Lawati has received his PhD in analytical chemistry from Universityof Hull, UK in 2007. Currently, he is employed at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science of the Sultan Qaboos University, Sultanate of Oman, as a professor. His research interests are focused on microfluidics, chemiluminescence, miniaturized analytical systems, chromatography and mass spectrometry.
